danger_signals.visual_form: Team Thornley characters in distinctive visual styling (wealthy/elite aesthetic in US localization)
danger_signals.meaning: Opposing faction; escalating threat level as player progresses through tournament
Rising stakes; each defeated opponent signals increased difficulty and danger from remaining competitors

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual fatigue or damage state during Fighting Scene events
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate danger of losing individual event; resource management required

danger_signals.visual_form: Limited inventory of power-ups available at shop (medal scarcity)
danger_signals.meaning: Economic danger; insufficient preparation for upcoming challenges

safety_signals.visual_form: Mall/store location accessible before each event
safety_signals.meaning: Opportunity to acquire power-ups and restore competitive advantage; player agency to mitigate risk

safety_signals.visual_form: Diverse competition formats (individual scoring vs. head-to-head elimination)
safety_signals.meaning: Variety of paths to victory; different team strengths can succeed in different event types

safety_signals.visual_form: Five characters per team with individual strengths/weaknesses
safety_signals.meaning: Strategic flexibility; players can select optimal roster for specific events

safety_signals.visual_form: Up to four-player local gameplay with alternating controller support
safety_signals.meaning: Collective safety through shared control and decision-making

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Blue-collar/working-class aesthetic; established as 'slobs from the Southside' in Todd's dialogue
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Team Thornley (wealthy elite variant); other high schools (Hanazono, Reiho in Japanese version)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Southside location; street-level competition venues; lower socioeconomic status signifiers

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Wealthy corporate sponsorship (Thornley Industries); 59th-floor corporate HQ headquarters location (US version)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Stylistically distinguished as elite/privileged; contrasts with Southside visual markers
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Corporate headquarters; high-end facilities; 'Hill' location (wealthy district); constructed colosseum (Japanese version)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: School-based identity; established Kunio-kun franchise characters (Double Dragon twins Ryuichi and Ryuji included)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Opposed by Reiho faction (Todo's school) and corporate-sponsored teams
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: School-affiliated competition; Japanese urban settings; traditional sports colosseum

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character portrait changes and dialogue scenes establishing class/social conflict; Todd's desperation vs. Crash's confidence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Todd issues challenge out of humiliation; Crash accepts with casual confidence; subordinates (Skip/Yamada) express concern about danger level

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Event-specific environmental changes (track field, pool, rooftop, fighting arena); visual shift from story cutscenes to gameplay arenas
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Teams cycle through events with individual performance metrics; elimination bracket narrows participant pool visually; character fatigue states reflect cumulative damage

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ending sequence: Thornley father congratulates Crash; Todd's subordinates reject his flowers in trash scene showing earned respect
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Team Thornley defects loyalty to Crash; Todd and Crash vow rematch on ice (hockey season teaser); social hierarchy inverted

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Localization context shift (US vs. Japanese version)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character redesigns (school uniform→tanktop/pants for Todd); setting changes (colosseum under construction→Thornley Industries exterior); corporate tower backdrop instead of sports facility
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Narrative emphasis shifts from school rivalry to class/socioeconomic conflict; Todd's father role changes from enabling parent to authoritarian corporate figure; threat scale escalates from singular rival to corporate team

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Shop interface displays available power-ups tied to medal count; visual inventory limitations communicate economic constraints
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Strategic purchasing decisions necessary; players must balance short-term advantages against long-term medal accumulation for final victory

environmental_storytelling.example: Southside represented as street-level, working-class venues vs. Thornley Industries 59th-floor corporate headquarters and constructed colosseum representing wealth/power
Todd redesigned from school uniform to tanktop/pants; visual styling distinguishes 'Hillers' (wealthy) from Southside 'slobs'; Team Thornley super-athletes visually distinct from student competitors

Events hosted at specific venues (pool, rooftop, fighting arena) that shift per story progression; colosseum in Japanese version vs. street challenge setting in localized version communicates different power structures

Cutscenes explicitly establish Todo's manipulation of father/Thornley's corporate sponsor team formation; Skip's warning about Southside danger; Todd's father's corporate pressure communicated through dialogue and setting
Ending scene shows Team Thornley rejecting Todd's reconciliation attempt by trashing flowers; visual gesture communicates earned respect and Todd's isolation despite nominal victory condition

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Issues challenge to Crash; manipulates father (Japanese) or appeals to corporate father (US) for competitive advantage; desperate emotional state reflected in dialogue urgency
Directs Team Thornley opposition; participant in head-to-head elimination events; visible competition against Crash's team
Attempts reconciliation scheme with Skip using flowers to create false narrative; subordinates reject him visibly; vows continued rivalry (ice hockey season teaser)
Accepts Todd's challenge casually; leads Southside team with implicit authority; projects working-class confidence
Competes across all event types; team follows his leadership; demonstrates dominance through repeated victories
Receives formal congratulations from corporate authority (Thornley father); doubts Todd's capacity for reconciliation; establishes cross-team respect (Team Thornley subordinates side with Crash)
Expresses concern about Southside danger; questions strategic necessity of street challenge; skeptical of Todd's planning
Initially follow corporate directive; respond to Crash's earned respect by rejecting Todd's reconciliation attempt; defect loyalty based on demonstrated character
Demands Todd prove himself through challenge victory; threatens expulsion for failure; demands respect through competitive excellence; corporate pressure drives tournament

notes: This game communicates world-state primarily through socioeconomic class contrast and tournament progression mechanics. The localization significantly reframes narrative from school-based Kunio-kun rivalry to working-class vs. corporate elite conflict. Danger signals emerge from tournament escalation and resource scarcity (medal economy). Faction identity is communicated through character appearance, dialogue, and environmental setting rather than explicit color/symbol systems typical of beat-em-ups. The ending subverts traditional victory narratives by showing earned respect from opposing team members, suggesting world-state transformation where class hierarchy is contested through athletic competition. Visual manifestation of world-state relies heavily on story cutscenes and dialogue rather than real-time environmental shifts during gameplay.
